content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 19, 2004

Heather C. McLaughlin
City Attorney
The City of Benicia
City Hall
250 East L Street
Benicia, CA 94510

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-04-225

Dear Ms. McLaughlin:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Mayor Steve Messina regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This advice is based on the facts you have provided in your request. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; section 83114.)

QUESTION

Does Mayor Steve Messina have a conflict of interest under the Act in making appointments to the design review commission and the planning commission where he has an ownership interest in a limited liability corporation that owns real property that is the subject of a pending application for development and that requires approval by the design review commission?

CONCLUSION

Assuming that Mayor Messina has no financial interest in any of the appointees, that there will be no understanding between the mayor and an appointee as to how the appointee will vote, and that steps will be taken to ensure that the potential appointee has not taken a position on the issue or expressed intentions as to how he or she might vote,

¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. All regulatory references are to Title 2 of the Code of Regulations unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-04-225
Page No. 2

the mayor's appointment of individuals to the vacant spots on the Design Review
Commission and the Planning Commission will not give rise to a conflict of interest.

FACTS

You have been authorized to seek advice on behalf of the City of Benicia's Mayor
Steve Messina. Mayor Messina has a one-third interest in a limited liability corporation
that owns an undeveloped parcel of real property located within the City of Benicia. The
estimated value of the property is approximately $390,000. An application has been
submitted to the city for development of the property as mixed use, with commercial
zoning First Street and residential above and to the rear. A previous project was
submitted to the city for approval but was eventually withdrawn.

The parties have now filed an application for the revised project and are funding
the required environmental review. The project will require a design review and an
accompanying environmental review, which is anticipated to be a mitigated negative
declaration. The project will comply with all city rules and regulations so that the only
city approvals, beyond building permits, will be a design review and an environmental
review. A design review and an environmental review will be conducted by the design
review commission. Any decision of the design review commission may be appealed to
the planning commission. Decisions of the planning commission may be appealed to the
Benicia City Council.

Under Government Code section 40605 the mayor has the power to appoint
commissioners subject to the approval of the city council. Because there are vacancies
on the commissions, the mayor is planning to make several appointments or
reappointments of either design review commissioners or planning commissioners in the
upcoming month.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.

In the McLaughlin Advice Letter, No. A-01-035, we advised that Mayor Messina
cannot participate, as a member of the city council, in hearing and deciding upon appeals
from planning commission decisions pertaining to the development of this real property.
In this request for advice, you have asked whether, despite his conflict of interest relating
to this property, the mayor may appoint members to the design review commission and
the planning commission.

File No. A-04-225
Page No. 3

As noted in the prior advice letter, the mayor is a public official and the decisions
to appoint members of the design review commission and the planning commission are
considered governmental decisions. This is not in dispute. Your question focuses on
step 6 of the conflict-of-interest analysis concerning foreseeability.

An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood
that it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect is only a mere
possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

In considering appointment decisions, we advised in the Lofgren Advice Letter,
No. A-96-042, that "[a]bsent a specific agreement between the mayor and the appointees,
it is not reasonably foreseeable that the appointment of the committee members will
affect [the mayor's] economic interests." (See also Dorsey Advice Letter, No. A-89-396;
Lofgren Advice Letter, No. A-86-307; Benjamin Advice Letter, No. A-86-148.)

In the McAndrews Advice Letter, No. A-99-213, we reviewed these and other
advice letters discussing the issue of foreseeability in the context of appointment
decisions and found that they concluded that "an appointment decision will not create a
conflict for an official in circumstances where:

"The official has no financial interest in the appointment decision;

"There is no understanding between the official and the appointee
as to how the appointee will vote; and

"The potential appointee has not taken a position on the issue or
otherwise expressed intentions as to how he or she might vote on
particular issues." (See also, Ungar Advice Letter, No. A-93-277;
McHugh Advice Letter, No. A-93-142.)

Additionally, as in those letters, our advice in this letter "is premised on the
assumption that an appointee will act independently, exercising his or her own judgment
to decide issues rather than merely carrying out the will of the appointing power."
(McAndrews, supra.)

Accordingly, if the appointments made by Mayor Messina meet the above
requirements² the mayor will not have a conflict of interest in the appointment of
individuals to the design review commission or the planning commission. Under these
circumstances, it is not reasonably foreseeable that his appointment of a particular
individual will have a material financial effect on his economic interests.

Of course, once the individuals are appointed, the mayor may not attempt to
influence the decisions of the either commission as to specific decisions where the mayor
would have a conflict of interest. (Lofgren Advice Letter, No. A-96-042; Dorsey, supra.)

² The official has no financial interest in the appointment decision if the persons appointed to the
positions are not sources of income, or sources of gifts to Mayor Messina.

File No. A-04-225
Page No. 4

Regulation 18702.3 describes when an official is using his official position to influence a
governmental decision:

"(a) With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an official's agency or an agency appointed by or subject to
the budgetary control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts
to influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts
by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer.

"(b) With regard to a governmental decision which is within or
before an agency not covered by subsection (a), the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the official
acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his
or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an
agency. Such actions include, but are not limited to the use of
official stationery."

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: William J. Lenkeit
Counsel, Legal Division

(Section 81001(b).)
